structured_choices.data_source: Hospital EHR database spanning 36 months (01/2021–12/2023), approximately 50,000 patient admission records. Each record includes: (1) clinical notes from the index admission (discharge summaries, progress notes, clinical assessments); (2) structured measurements (vital signs, lab results, comorbidity codes, medication lists); (3) 30-day readmission label (binary: readmitted yes/no within 30 days of discharge). Data quality: missing modalities (text or measurements) will trigger record exclusion; missing values within present modalities handled via modality-specific strategies (see preprocessing).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: readmission event within 30 calendar days of index discharge date, defined from hospital administrative records (readmission flag in EHR). Records with discharge date within 30 days of data cutoff (12/31/2023) are excluded to ensure complete label ascertainment.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split stratified by patient demographics (age group: <40, 40–65, >65; sex; admission type: emergency/planned). Reasoning: (1) temporal split prevents information leakage and matches deployment setting where future patient records are predicted; (2) stratification ensures each split reflects realistic population composition and prevents confound imbalance across train/val/test. Allocation: months 1–22 (first 22 months) for training (n≈35,000); months 23–28 (6 months) for validation (n≈8,000); months 29–36 (final 8 months) for test (n≈7,000). Within-split stratification ensures each segment preserves age, sex, and admission-type distributions to ~±3%.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers (text tokenization vocabulary, scaling parameters, imputation statistics, embedding weights if pre-trained) are fit exclusively on the training split (months 1–22). Fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation and test splits. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into model training. Text preprocessing (lowercasing, punctuation removal, tokenization) uses a vocabulary derived from training text only; out-of-vocabulary tokens in validation/test are mapped to <UNK>.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: age, sex, admission type, length of stay, primary diagnosis code (ICD-10), comorbidity count (Charlson index components), vital signs at admission (BP, HR, O2 sat, temperature), baseline laboratory values (creatinine, hemoglobin, WBC, glucose), medication count, prior 12-month admission count. Text features: clinical notes processed via pre-trained BioBERT (or clinical BERT if available) to produce contextual token embeddings; document-level representation via mean pooling of token embeddings, concatenated with a separate TF-IDF sparse representation of high-variance clinical terms (computed on training text). Structured and text embeddings are concatenated into a single feature vector. No features encode the label; temporal features (discharge date relative to data cutoff) are included to capture temporal trend but are not future information.
structured_choices.model: Multi-modal deep neural network: (1) Structured branch: 2–3 fully connected hidden layers (128–256 units, ReLU) with batch normalization and dropout (p=0.3); (2) Text branch: pre-trained BioBERT encoder (frozen or fine-tuned, determined via validation) followed by pooling and 1–2 dense layers (128 units, ReLU, dropout p=0.3); (3) Fusion layer: concatenation of structured and text representations, followed by 2 dense layers (64 units, ReLU) and sigmoid output. Hyperparameters (layer sizes, dropout, learning rate, epochs) selected via grid search on validation set (see validation approach). Loss function: binary cross-entropy with class-weight balancing if imbalance exists.
structured_choices.baseline: Structured-only logistic regression, trained on the same structured feature set, optimized with identical effort: hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength L1/L2, solver) performed via grid search on the same validation set using the same primary metric. This ensures fair comparison: the baseline receives equivalent computational and tuning investment and operates under identical data conditions. A secondary baseline (simple heuristic: e.g., readmission probability proportional to prior 12-month admission count) is included to establish a lower bound. The multi-modal model must outperform both baselines to support the hypothesis.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Justification: (1) binary readmission labels are expected to exhibit class imbalance (readmission typically <30% in hospital populations), rendering ROC-AUC insensitive to false positive rate changes; (2) from a clinical operations perspective, false positives (flagging low-risk patients) waste intervention resources, while false negatives (missing high-risk patients) cause preventable harm—AUPRC captures this trade-off more directly than accuracy; (3) stakeholder objective is to improve readmission *prediction error*, which AUPRC quantifies across thresholds; (4) decision threshold can be calibrated post-hoc based on resource constraints.
secondary_metrics: F1-score at optimal threshold (decision support clarity)
Sensitivity and Specificity at the validation-optimized threshold (clinical operability)
Calibration error (expected calibration error, ECE) to assess probability validity for risk stratification
Brier score (overall probabilistic accuracy, sensitive to both discrimination and calibration)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 23–28) is used exclusively for hyperparameter selection and model selection. AUPRC is computed on validation data after each hyperparameter configuration; the configuration yielding the highest validation AUPRC is selected. Early stopping (if training via mini-batch gradient descent) monitored on validation AUPRC to prevent overfitting. The test set is held out and never inspected during model development.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (months 29–36) is evaluated exactly once, after model and hyperparameters are frozen. All primary and secondary metrics are computed on test data to generate the final performance report. No threshold tuning, model retraining, or architecture changes are performed after test evaluation. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap) to account for finite sample uncertainty.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Patient demographic confounds (age, sex) are addressed via stratified splits to ensure balanced representation across train/val/test. Clinical severity confounds (comorbidity burden, admission type) are included as explicit features in both the multi-modal model and baseline, allowing the model to account for heterogeneous risk. Temporal confounds (changes in treatment practices over 36 months) are mitigated via temporal split: the test set represents the most recent 8 months, reflecting current practice; validation uses the preceding 6 months to tune for recent data distribution. To assess robustness to temporal shift, a secondary analysis performs stratified evaluation on test data split into early (months 29–32) and late (months 33–36) subsets, checking for performance degradation that would indicate temporal non-stationarity. Multi-site confounds are not present (single hospital); generalization to other sites is outside the scope of this experiment and flagged as a limitation.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Objective and Scope

This experiment tests whether a multi-modal deep learning model integrating clinical text and structured measurements produces lower readmission prediction error than a structured-only baseline. Success is defined as statistically significant improvement in AUPRC on a held-out test set. The experiment does not establish generalization across hospital systems; that requires a separate validation study.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling

Data are drawn from a single hospital's EHR database spanning 36 months (January 2021 through December 2023), capturing approximately 50,000 patient admissions. Each admission record includes: (a) narrative clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, clinical assessments), (b) structured measurements (vital signs, laboratory results, comorbidity codes, medications), and (c) a binary readmission label derived from administrative records (readmitted within 30 days of discharge: yes/no). Records are excluded if discharge occurred within 30 days of the data cutoff (to ensure complete label ascertainment) or if both text and structured data are absent; records with data missing in only one modality are retained and handled via modality-specific imputation (see preprocessing).

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Split

A **temporal stratified split** is used:
- **Training (months 1–22):** ~35,000 records
- **Validation (months 23–28):** ~8,000 records  
- **Test (months 29–36):** ~7,000 records

**Rationale for temporal split:** In deployment, the model predicts readmission for prospective (future) admissions. A temporal split mirrors this setting and prevents information leakage—training data strictly precedes validation and test data. This avoids the artifact of learning from future patient information.

**Stratification within each temporal segment** ensures that age group (<40, 40–65, >65), sex, and admission type (emergency/planned) distributions are balanced across splits to ±3%. Stratification prevents demographic confounds from skewing comparisons and ensures the test set represents the true population seen at deployment.

### 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (vocabulary, scaling parameters, embedding models) are **fit on training data only** and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into the training process.

**Structured features** include: age, sex, admission type, length of stay, primary diagnosis (ICD-10), comorbidity count (Charlson index components), vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen saturation, temperature), baseline labs (creatinine, hemoglobin, WBC, glucose), medication count, and 12-month prior admission history. Missing structured values are imputed using median (continuous) or mode (categorical) computed from training data only.

**Text features** are extracted using a pre-trained clinical BERT model (e.g., BioBERT or SciBERT) applied to discharge summaries and clinical notes. Token embeddings are pooled into a document-level representation via mean pooling, then concatenated with a TF-IDF sparse representation of high-variance clinical terms (vocabulary and IDF statistics fit on training text). Text and structured representations are concatenated into a unified feature vector.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Multi-modal model:** A neural network with separate branches for structured and text data. The structured branch consists of 2–3 fully connected layers (128–256 units, ReLU activation) with batch normalization and dropout (p=0.3). The text branch uses the pre-trained BERT encoder (optionally fine-tuned) followed by pooling and 1–2 dense layers (128 units, ReLU). A fusion layer concatenates the two representations and passes them through 2 additional dense layers (64 units) to a sigmoid output (binary classification). Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set.

**Baseline:** A logistic regression model trained on structured features only, using the same structured feature engineering pipeline. Crucially, the baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameters (L1/L2 regularization, solver) are selected via the same validation procedure and the same primary metric (AUPRC). This ensures a fair comparison; the hypothesis is falsified only if the multi-modal model outperforms the structured baseline despite both receiving commensurate optimization.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (months 23–28) is used exclusively to select hyperparameters and the final model. For each hyperparameter configuration, AUPRC is computed on the validation set. The configuration maximizing validation AUPRC is selected. Early stopping (if used) is monitored on validation AUPRC. **The test set is never inspected during development.**

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC).** Readmission is typically a rare event (base rate <30%), rendering ROC-AUC insensitive to operating-point changes. AUPRC directly quantifies the precision-recall trade-off, which aligns with clinical operations: false positives waste intervention resources, while false negatives cause preventable readmissions. AUPRC reflects the stakeholder objective of improving readmission prediction accuracy.

**Secondary metrics:** F1-score at the validation-optimized threshold, sensitivity and specificity, calibration error (ECE), and Brier score. These provide a complete picture of model performance and clinical utility.

### 8. Test Evaluation and Reporting

After model selection, the test set (months 29–36) is evaluated **exactly once**. No retraining, threshold tuning, or architecture changes occur after test evaluation. Primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap) to quantify uncertainty.

### 9. Confound Control and Robustness Checks

**Demographic confounds** (age, sex, admission type) are included as explicit features in both the multi-modal and baseline models and balanced across splits via stratification.

**Temporal confounds** are addressed via the temporal split and a secondary robustness check: the test set is further stratified into early (months 29–32) and late (months 33–36) subsets to assess whether performance degrades over time (indicating temporal non-stationarity).

**Assumption scoping:** This experiment establishes relative performance at a single hospital over 36 months. It does not establish: (1) generalization to other hospitals or patient populations, (2) causality (the multi-modal model may simply exploit confounds better), or (3) clinical net benefit (improved prediction alone does not guarantee better outcomes without a randomized intervention trial). These remain open questions for follow-on work.
